sourceQuestion:
Suppose that L_0 is a regular language and L_1 is a context-free language. Which one of the following languages is NOT necessarily context-free?

A. L_1 \ L_0
B. L_1 . L_0
C. L_1 - L_0
D. L_0 - L_1

parsed.stem: Suppose that L_0 is a regular language and L_1 is a context-free language. Which one of the following languages is NOT necessarily context-free?
For a string w, we define w^R to be the reverse of w. For example, if w = 01101 then w^R = 10110. Which of the following languages is/are context-free?

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following languages:
L_1 = {ww | w in {a,b}^*}
L_2 = {a^m b^n c^n | m >= 1, n >= 0}

Which of the following statements is/are FALSE?

A. L_1 is context-free but L_2 is deterministic context-free
B. Neither L_1 nor L_2 is context-free
C. L_1 is not context-free but L_2 is context-free
D. Both L_1 and L_2 are context-free
sourceAnswer: A. L_1 is context-free but L_2 is deterministic context-free

options.text: L_1 is context-free but L_2 is deterministic context-free
Neither L_1 nor L_2 is context-free
L_1 is not context-free but L_2 is context-free
Both L_1 and L_2 are context-free

sourceAnswer: A. The language generated by G is {a^(m+n) b^n | m >= 0, n >= 0}

parsed.stem:
Consider the context-free grammar G below:
  S -> aSb | X
  X -> aX | b
where S and X are non-terminals, and a and b are terminal symbols. The starting non-terminal is S.

Which one of the following statements is CORRECT?

options.text: The language generated by G is {a^(m+n) b^n | m >= 0, n >= 0}
The language generated by G is {a^(n+1) b^n | n >= 0}
The language generated by G is {a^n b^(n+1) | n >= 0}
The language generated by G is not a regular language

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following two languages over the alphabet {a, b, c}, where m and n are natural numbers.
  L1 = {a^m b^n c^(m+n) | m, n >= 1}
  L2 = {a^m b^n c^(mn) | m, n >= 1}

Which ONE of the following statements is CORRECT?
A. Both L1 and L2 are context-free languages.
B. L1 is a context-free language but L2 is not a context-free language.
C. L1 is not a context-free language but L2 is a context-free language.
D. Neither L1 nor L2 is a context-free language.
sourceAnswer: B. L1 is a context-free language but L2 is not a context-free language.

options.text: Both L1 and L2 are context-free languages.
L1 is a context-free language but L2 is not a context-free language.
L1 is not a context-free language but L2 is a context-free language.
Neither L1 nor L2 is a context-free language.

parsed.stem:
In the context-free grammar below, S is the start symbol, a and b are terminals, and epsilon denotes the empty string.
  S -> aSAb | epsilon
  A -> bA | b

The grammar generates the language

sourceQuestion:
In the context-free grammar below, S is the start symbol, a and b are terminals, and e denotes the empty string.

S -> aSb | bSa | ab | e

Which of the following strings is NOT generated by the grammar?

A. aabb
B. bab
C. abba
D. bababab

parsed.stem:
In the context-free grammar below, S is the start symbol, a and b are terminals, and e denotes the empty string.

S -> aSb | bSa | ab | e

Which of the following strings is NOT generated by the grammar?

sourceQuestion:
The two grammars given below generate a language over the alphabet {x, y, z}.

G1: S -> x | xS | yB
     B -> y | yB | zS | z

G2: S -> y | yS | zS | xB
     B -> zB | z

Which one of the following choices describes the properties satisfied by the strings in these languages?

A. G1: No y appears before any x;  G2: Every x is followed by at least one z
B. G1: No y appears before any x;  G2: No z appears before any x
C. G1: No z appears before any x;  G2: No z appears before any x
D. G1: No z appears before any y;  G2: No z appears before any x
sourceAnswer: D. G1: No z appears before any y; G2: No z appears before any x

parsed.stem:
The two grammars given below generate a language over the alphabet {x, y, z}.

G1: S -> x | xS | yB
     B -> y | yB | zS | z

G2: S -> y | yS | zS | xB
     B -> zB | z

Which one of the following choices describes the properties satisfied by the strings in these languages?

options.text: G1: No y appears before any x; G2: Every x is followed by at least one z
G1: No y appears before any x; G2: No z appears before any x
G1: No z appears before any x; G2: No z appears before any x
G1: No z appears before any y; G2: No z appears before any x

sourceQuestion:
Consider the grammar given below:

S -> xB | yA
A -> x | xS | yAA
B -> y | yS | xBB

Consider the following strings.
i.   xxyyx
ii.  xyyxy
iii. xyxxy
iv.  yxxy
v.   yxx
vi.  xyxy

Which of the above strings are generated by the grammar?

A. i, ii and iii
B. ii, v and vi
C. i, iii and iv
D. iii and iv

parsed.stem:
Consider the grammar given below:

S -> xB | yA
A -> x | xS | yAA
B -> y | yS | xBB

i.   xxyyx
ii.  xyyxy
iii. xyxxy
iv.  yxxy
v.   yxx
vi.  xyxy

Which of the above strings are generated by the grammar?

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following grammars. Names representing terminals have been specified in capital letters.

G1:
  stmnt -> WHILE (expr) stmnt
  stmnt -> OTHER

G2:
  stmnt -> WHILE (expr) stmnt
  stmnt -> OTHER
  expr -> ID
  expr -> expr OP ID

Which one of the following statements is true?

A. G1 is context-free but not regular and G2 is regular
B. G1 is context-free but not regular and G2 is context-free but not regular
C. Both G1 and G2 are regular
D. Both G1 and G2 are context-free but neither of them is regular
sourceAnswer: D. Both G1 and G2 are context-free but neither of them is regular

parsed.stem:
Consider the following grammars. Names representing terminals have been specified in capital letters.

G1:
  stmnt -> WHILE (expr) stmnt
  stmnt -> OTHER

G2:
  stmnt -> WHILE (expr) stmnt
  stmnt -> OTHER
  expr -> ID
  expr -> expr OP ID

Which one of the following statements is true?

options.text: G1 is context-free but not regular and G2 is regular
G1 is context-free but not regular and G2 is context-free but not regular
Both G1 and G2 are regular
Both G1 and G2 are context-free but neither of them is regular

parsed.stem:
Consider a CFG with the following productions.

S -> AA | B
A -> 0A | A0 | 1
B -> 0B0 | 1

S is the start symbol, A and B are non-terminals and 0 and 1 are the terminals. The language generated by this grammar is:

options.text: The set of all strings over {0, 1} containing at least two 1's

sourceQuestion:
Given Sigma = {a, b}, which one of the following sets is not countable?

A. Set of all strings over Sigma
B. Set of all languages over Sigma
C. Set of all regular languages over Sigma
D. Set of all languages over Sigma accepted by Turing machines
sourceAnswer: B. Set of all languages over Sigma

parsed.stem: Given Sigma = {a, b}, which one of the following sets is not countable?

options.text: Set of all strings over Sigma
Set of all languages over Sigma
Set of all regular languages over Sigma
Set of all languages over Sigma accepted by Turing machines

sourceQuestion:
Let Sigma be a finite non-empty alphabet and let 2^(Sigma*) be the power set of Sigma*. Which one of the following is TRUE?

A. Both Sigma* and 2^(Sigma*) are countable
B. Sigma* is countable and 2^(Sigma*) is uncountable
C. Sigma* is uncountable and 2^(Sigma*) is countable
D. Both Sigma* and 2^(Sigma*) are uncountable
sourceAnswer: B. Sigma* is countable and 2^(Sigma*) is uncountable

parsed.stem: Let Sigma be a finite non-empty alphabet and let 2^(Sigma*) be the power set of Sigma*. Which one of the following is TRUE?

options.text: Sigma* is countable and 2^(Sigma*) is uncountable
Sigma* is uncountable and 2^(Sigma*) is countable

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following problems are undecidable?
A. Membership problem in context-free languages.
B. Whether a given context-free language is regular.
C. Whether a finite state automaton halts on all inputs.
D. Membership problem for type 0 languages.

parsed.stem: Which of the following problems are undecidable?

options.text: Whether a given context-free language is regular.
Whether a finite state automaton halts on all inputs.

sourceQuestion:
It is undecidable whether:
A. An arbitrary Turing machine halts after 100 steps.
B. A Turing machine prints a specific letter.
C. A Turing machine computes the products of two numbers.
D. None of the above.

parsed.stem: It is undecidable whether:

options.text: An arbitrary Turing machine halts after 100 steps.
A Turing machine prints a specific letter.
A Turing machine computes the products of two numbers.

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following statements is false?
A. The Halting Problem of Turing machines is undecidable
B. Determining whether a context-free grammar is ambiguous is undecidable
C. Given two arbitrary context-free grammars G_1 and G_2 it is undecidable whether L(G_1) = L(G_2)
D. Given two regular grammars G_1 and G_2 it is undecidable whether L(G_1) = L(G_2)
sourceAnswer: D. Given two regular grammars G_1 and G_2 it is undecidable whether L(G_1) = L(G_2)

parsed.stem: Which of the following statements is false?

options.text: The Halting Problem of Turing machines is undecidable
Determining whether a context-free grammar is ambiguous is undecidable
Given two arbitrary context-free grammars G_1 and G_2 it is undecidable whether L(G_1) = L(G_2)
Given two regular grammars G_1 and G_2 it is undecidable whether L(G_1) = L(G_2)

sourceQuestion:
Which one of the following is not decidable?
A. Given a Turing machine M, a string w and an integer k, M accepts w within k steps
B. Equivalence of two given Turing machines
C. Language accepted by a given finite state machine is not empty
D. Language generated by a context free grammar is non-empty
sourceAnswer: B. Equivalence of two given Turing machines

parsed.stem: Which one of the following is not decidable?

options.text: Given a Turing machine M, a string w and an integer k, M accepts w within k steps
Equivalence of two given Turing machines
Language accepted by a given finite state machine is not empty
Language generated by a context free grammar is non-empty

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following decision problems:
(P1): Does a given finite state machine accept a given string?
(P2): Does a given context free grammar generate an infinite number of strings?
Which of the following statements is true?
A. Both (P1) and (P2) are decidable
B. Neither (P1) nor (P2) is decidable
C. Only (P1) is decidable
D. Only (P2) is decidable

parsed.stem:
Consider the following decision problems:
(P1): Does a given finite state machine accept a given string?
(P2): Does a given context free grammar generate an infinite number of strings?
Which of the following statements is true?

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following problem X. Given a Turing machine M over the input alphabet Sigma, any state q of M and a word w in Sigma*, does the computation of M on w visit the state q?
Which of the following statements about X is correct?
A. X is decidable
B. X is undecidable but partially decidable
C. X is undecidable and not even partially decidable
D. X is not a decision problem
sourceAnswer: B. X is undecidable but partially decidable

parsed.stem:
Consider the following problem X. Given a Turing machine M over the input alphabet Sigma, any state q of M and a word w in Sigma*, does the computation of M on w visit the state q?
Which of the following statements about X is correct?

options.text: X is undecidable but partially decidable
X is undecidable and not even partially decidable
X is not a decision problem

sourceQuestion:
Consider two languages L_1 and L_2 each on the alphabet Sigma. Let f : Sigma* -> Sigma* be a polynomial time computable bijection such that (for all x) x in L_1 iff f(x) in L_2. Further, let f^(-1) be also polynomial time computable. Which of the following CANNOT be true?
A. L_2 in P and L_1 is finite
B. L_1 not in NP and L_2 in P
C. L_1 is undecidable and L_2 is decidable
D. L_1 is recursively enumerable and L_2 is recursive
sourceAnswer: C. L_1 is undecidable and L_2 is decidable

parsed.stem: Consider two languages L_1 and L_2 each on the alphabet Sigma. Let f : Sigma* -> Sigma* be a polynomial time computable bijection such that (for all x) x in L_1 iff f(x) in L_2. Further, let f^(-1) be also polynomial time computable. Which of the following CANNOT be true?

options.text: L_2 in P and L_1 is finite
L_1 not in NP and L_2 in P
L_1 is undecidable and L_2 is decidable
L_1 is recursively enumerable and L_2 is recursive

sourceQuestion:
A single tape Turing Machine M has two states q0 and q1, of which q0 is the starting state. The tape alphabet of M is {0, 1, B} and its input alphabet is {0, 1}. The symbol B is the blank symbol used to indicate end of an input string. The transition function of M is described in the following table:

       0          1          B
q0: (q1,1,R)  (q1,0,R)  (q1,1,R)
q1: (q0,1,L)  (q0,0,L)  ---

The table is interpreted as: entry (q1,1,R) in row q0, column 0 means that if M is in state q0 reading 0, it moves to state q1, writes 1, and moves Right.

Which of the following statements about M is/are TRUE?
A. M does not halt on any string in {0,1}*
B. M does not halt on any string in (0+1)*
C. M halts on all strings ending in a 0
D. M halts on all strings ending in 1
sourceAnswer: A. M does not halt on any string in {0,1}*

parsed.stem:

q1: (q0,1,L)  (q0,0,L)  ---

The table is interpreted as: entry (q1,1,R) in row q0, column 0 means that if M is in state q0 reading 0, it moves to state q1, writes 1, and moves Right.

Which of the following statements about M is/are TRUE?

options.text: M does not halt on any string in {0,1}*
M does not halt on any string in (0+1)*
M halts on all strings ending in a 0
M halts on all strings ending in 1

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following problems is undecidable?
A. Membership problem for CFGs
B. Ambiguity problem for CFGs
C. Finiteness problem for FSAs
D. Equivalence problem for FSAs

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following are decidable?
I. Whether the intersection of two regular languages is infinite
II. Whether a given context-free language is regular
III. Whether two push-down automata accept the same language
IV. Whether a given grammar is context-free

A. I and II
B. I and IV
C. II and III
D. II and IV

parsed.stem:
Which of the following are decidable?
I. Whether the intersection of two regular languages is infinite
II. Whether a given context-free language is regular
III. Whether two push-down automata accept the same language
IV. Whether a given grammar is context-free

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following problems are decidable?
1. Does a given program ever produce an output?
2. If L is a context-free language, then is L' (complement of L) also context-free?
3. If L is a regular language, then is L also regular?
4. If L is a recursive language, then is L' (complement) also recursive?

A. 2 only
B. 3 only
C. 2 and 4 only
D. 3 and 4 only

parsed.stem:
Which of the following problems are decidable?
1. Does a given program ever produce an output?
2. If L is a context-free language, then is L' (complement of L) also context-free?
3. If L is a regular language, then is L also regular?
4. If L is a recursive language, then is L' (complement) also recursive?

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following is/are undecidable?
1. G is a CFG. Is L(G) = phi?
2. G is a CFG. Is L(G) = Sigma*?
3. M is a Turing machine. Is L(M) regular?
4. A is a DFA and N is an NFA. Is L(A) = L(N)?

A. 3 only
B. 3 and 4 only
C. 1, 2 and 3 only
D. 2 and 3 only

parsed.stem:
Which of the following is/are undecidable?
1. G is a CFG. Is L(G) = phi?
2. G is a CFG. Is L(G) = Sigma*?
3. M is a Turing machine. Is L(M) regular?
4. A is a DFA and N is an NFA. Is L(A) = L(N)?

sourceQuestion:
Which one of the following problems is undecidable?

A. Deciding if a given context-free grammar is ambiguous.
B. Deciding if a given string is generated by a given context-free grammar.
C. Deciding if the language generated by a given context-free grammar is empty.
D. Deciding if the language generated by a given context-free grammar is finite.
sourceAnswer: A. Deciding if a given context-free grammar is ambiguous.

parsed.stem: Which one of the following problems is undecidable?

options.text: Deciding if a given context-free grammar is ambiguous.
Deciding if a given string is generated by a given context-free grammar.
Deciding if the language generated by a given context-free grammar is empty.
Deciding if the language generated by a given context-free grammar is finite.

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following statements.
I. The complement of every Turing decidable language is Turing decidable.
II. There exists some language which is in NP but is not Turing decidable.
III. If L is a language in NP, L is Turing decidable.

Which of the above statements is/are true?

A. Only II
B. Only III
C. Only I and III
D. Only II and III

parsed.stem:
Consider the following statements.
I. The complement of every Turing decidable language is Turing decidable.
II. There exists some language which is in NP but is not Turing decidable.
III. If L is a language in NP, L is Turing decidable.

Which of the above statements is/are true?

sourceQuestion:
Language L_1 is polynomial time reducible to language L_2. Language L_2 is polynomial time reducible to language L_3, which in turn polynomial time reducible to language L_1.

Which of the following is/are true?
I. If L_1 is in P then L_2 is in P
II. If L_2 is in NP then L_1 is in NP
III. If L_2 is in NP then L_3 is in NP
IV. L_1, L_2, L_3 are NP-complete

A. II only
B. III only
C. I and IV only
D. I only

parsed.stem:
Language L_1 is polynomial time reducible to language L_2. Language L_2 is polynomial time reducible to language L_3, which in turn polynomial time reducible to language L_1.

Which of the following is/are true?
I. If L_1 is in P then L_2 is in P
II. If L_2 is in NP then L_1 is in NP
III. If L_2 is in NP then L_3 is in NP
IV. L_1, L_2, L_3 are NP-complete

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following decision problems are undecidable?
I. Given NFAs N_1 and N_2, is L(N_1) ∩ L(N_2) = ∅?
II. Given a CFG G and a string w, does w ∈ L(G)?
III. Given CFGs G_1 and G_2, is L(G_1) ∩ L(G_2) = ∅?
IV. Given a TM M, is L(M) = ∅?

A. I and IV only
B. II and III only
C. III and IV only
D. II and IV only

II. Given a CFG G and a string w, does w ∈ L(G)?
III. Given CFGs G_1 and G_2, is L(G_1) ∩ L(G_2) = ∅?
